mnemonics.title: Two's Complement Conversion
mnemonics.content: TWIST: Take the binary, Write it, Invert the bits, Switch (add 1), Throw away carry. Or: 'Copy from right until you find a 1, then flip all bits to the left'.

mnemonics.title: Overflow Detection
mnemonics.content: OVERBLOW: OVerflow occurs when signs aRe Both the saME and the result is diffErent. Remember: Same Sign + Different Result = Overflow.

mnemonics.title: Endianness
mnemonics.content: LITTLE-END: Little End stores Least Significant Byte first. BIG-END: Big End stores Biggest byte first (most significant).

acronyms.usage: Represents each decimal digit with 4 binary bits (0-9)
The leftmost bit in binary, often used as sign bit in signed representations
The rightmost bit in binary, represents smallest place value

acronyms.expansion: American Standard Code for Information Interchange
Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers

acronyms.usage: Another name for little-endian byte ordering

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a circular number wheel from 0 to 255 (for 8-bit). Going backward from 0 wraps to 255 (representing -1), then 254 (-2), etc. Two's complement is like taking the complement path going backward.
Draw a number line. Unsigned: 0 to 2^n - 1. Signed two's complement: -2^(n-1) to 2^(n-1) - 1. Notice the negative side has one extra value (-128 for 8-bit).

Think of a thermometer: overflow happens when you go past the top and wrap to below zero. Positive overflow = goes past max and becomes negative. Negative overflow = goes past min and becomes positive.

analogies.analogy: Think of a car's odometer going backwards from 00000: 99999 represents -1, 99998 represents -2. This is exactly how two's complement works - it's like modular arithmetic on a fixed number of digits.

Think of money: $123.45 always has exactly 2 decimal places. Fixed-point is similar - you decide in advance where the decimal point goes, then do all arithmetic with integers and remember the implied decimal position.

Think of adding zeros to the left of a number like adding leading zeros to a check - you're preserving the value while changing the 'paper size'. For negative two's complement, adding 1s on left is like adding more 'debt' (sign bits) to a bigger canvas.

Writing phone number '12-34-56' on envelope: big-endian writes '123456' starting from most significant (left). Little-endian writes it reversed for machine processing. Both represent the same number, just different byte ordering.
